narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually above the typical student's 3.21—and your LSAT score of 36, close to the average of 36.7. Your plan to attend full-time aligns with most students, and your lower-middle family income reflects challenges many from similar backgrounds face. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 51.8% chance you'll fail the bar—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar readiness. Countering that somewhat was your LSAT score, which nudged the prediction toward passing, as did your stronger undergraduate GPA, both showing potential the model recognized. Your Year 1 decile of 2 (versus an average 5.71) had a smaller but still negative pull toward failure, while your full-time status and family income offered minor positives. Compared to typical students who pass, your mid-tier test scores and enrollment choice are competitive, but the low Year 3 decile stood out as the dominant hurdle, more influential than anything else. This isn't a judgment on your resilience or drive—many from underrepresented backgrounds like yours overcome similar stats through targeted growth. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent academic decile, perhaps by excelling in upper-level courses or prep programs, and consider retaking the LSAT to strengthen that edge. Law schools value persistence, and with strategic steps, you can reapply stronger next cycle. We're rooting for you.
